claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and the children you're asking about.

Let me start with: **Do you have a child or children that you're responsible for?** If yes, could you tell me how many children you have and their ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex, who's 14, and Blake, who's 19.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have Alex (14 years old) and Blake (19 years old).

Let me ask about Blake first, since age is an important factor:

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training?** For example, is Blake enrolled in full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels, Scottish Highers, or equivalent) or an approved training program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is in approved education - studying for A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Since Blake is 19 and in approved education, I need to check a few more details.

**When did Blake turn 19?** I need to know the specific date or at least the month and year when Blake turned 19, as there are time limits that apply once a young person reaches 19 while in education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I'm not entirely sure of the exact date off the top of my head - it was earlier this year. Is that going to be a problem?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
No problem at all - I understand you might not have the exact date right away. Let me ask this in a different way that might help:

**Can you tell me approximately which month Blake turned 19 this year?** For example, was it in the early part of the year (January-March), spring (April-June), or more recently?

The reason this matters is that Child Benefit for someone over 19 can only continue until a specific point in their education, so the timing is important for Blake's eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It was around February or March, I think - somewhere in that timeframe.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful to know Blake turned 19 around February or March this year.

Now, for young people who turn 19 while in approved education, Child Benefit can only continue until a certain point. Specifically, it stops on the **31st August following their 19th birthday** (or when they leave education, if that's earlier).

Since Blake turned 19 around February/March this year, the benefit would have stopped on 31st August of this year, which has now passed.

Let me now ask about Alex, your 14-year-old:

**Does Alex live with you, or do they live with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me - they live with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. This is an important factor.

**A few questions about Alex's living situation:**

1. **Who does Alex spend most of their time with** - is it primarily with your ex-partner?

2. **Do you contribute to Alex's upkeep** - for example, do you provide financial support or contribute to their care in any way?

3. **Is your ex-partner claiming Child Benefit for Alex**, or do you know if anyone else is claiming it?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with my ex-partner full-time, so they spend most of their time there. I'm not currently contributing financially - things have been tight for me. And yes, my ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me make sure I understand the full picture:

- **Alex (14)**: Lives full-time with your ex-partner, who is already claiming Child Benefit for them, and you don't currently contribute financially to Alex's upkeep.
- **Blake (19)**: Turned 19 around February/March this year and is studying A-levels at sixth form.

Before I give you my assessment, let me ask one final question: **Are you currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or have you been receiving it recently?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I haven't been claiming it, no. That's why I'm asking now - I wanted to see if I'm eligible to claim for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## My Assessment

I'm afraid **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake**. Here's why:

**For Alex (age 14):**
- While Alex meets the basic age requirement (under 16), your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for them
- Under UK rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any time - typically the person the child lives with or who has main responsibility
- Since Alex lives full-time with your ex-partner and you're not currently contributing to their upkeep, your ex-partner has the entitlement, not you

**For Blake (age 19):**
- Although Blake is in approved education (A-levels), there's an age limit that applies
- Child Benefit for young people in education stops on **31st August following their 19th birthday**
- Since Blake turned 19 around February/March this year, their eligibility ended on 31st August this year
- As that date has now passed, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake, even though they're still studying

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If your circumstances change in the future - for example, if Alex were to come and live with you - that would be worth reassessing. Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about these rules?

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old and under 16, so meets the basic age requirement for Child Benefit. However, Alex lives full-time with your ex-partner, who is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time - typically the person the child lives with or who has main responsibility for them. Since your ex-partner is already claiming and Alex lives with them full-time, and you are not contributing to Alex's upkeep, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and is in approved education (studying A-levels). Child Benefit can continue for young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training, but it stops on 31st August following their 19th birthday (or when they leave education, whichever is earlier). Since Blake turned 19 around February/March of this year, Child Benefit eligibility would have ended on 31st August of this year. As that date has now passed, you are no longer eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, even though they are still in education.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either child. For Alex (14), the ex-partner is already claiming and has main responsibility as Alex lives with them full-time. For Blake (19), the age limit has been exceeded - Child Benefit stops on 31st August following a young person's 19th birthday, which has already passed since Blake turned 19 in February/March of this year.
